title: Classes and private state

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Classes and private state; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Classes and private state
sources.claim: JavaScript class syntax defines constructor and prototype methods with private fields and inheritance rules over the existing object model.

sources.claim: JavaScript class syntax defines constructor and prototype methods with private fields and inheritance rules over the existing object model.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JavaScript class syntax defines constructor and prototype methods with private fields and inheritance rules over the existing object model.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Counter private field changes only through methods that preserve its nonnegative invariant. Your goal is to implement and test a class that preserves an invariant. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Object records and property access; Function declarations, expressions and arrows; Prototype delegation; Parameters, rest and return contracts. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: JavaScript class syntax defines constructor and prototype methods with private fields and inheritance rules over the existing object model. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Classes are strict, methods are not auto-bound, private names are lexical, and derived constructors must initialize super first. This draft therefore rejects: Class methods automatically retain their instance this when passed as callbacks.

includes: Use class syntax, constructors, methods and private fields for justified stateful abstractions.
Implement and test a class that preserves an invariant.
excludes: Classes are strict, methods are not auto-bound, private names are lexical, and derived constructors must initialize super first.

outcomes: Implement and test a class that preserves an invariant.

examples.title: Classes and private state: concrete case
examples.situation: A Counter private field changes only through methods that preserve its nonnegative invariant.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A Counter private field changes only through methods that preserve its nonnegative invariant.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Methods are not auto-bound; preserve the receiver, bind explicitly, or use an intentional instance arrow field.
examples.result: The result follows because Methods are not auto-bound; preserve the receiver, bind explicitly, or use an intentional instance arrow field.

examples.title: Classes and private state: boundary case
examples.situation: Classes are strict, methods are not auto-bound, private names are lexical, and derived constructors must initialize super first.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Classes are strict, methods are not auto-bound, private names are lexical, and derived constructors must initialize super first.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Class methods automatically retain their instance this when passed as callbacks.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Methods are not auto-bound; preserve the receiver, bind explicitly, or use an intentional instance arrow field.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Classes and private state, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Classes and private state before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
